title: Willow melon basket
subtitle: A round wide-topped basket for display and carrying
excerpt: A willow melon basket with a circular base, straight-sided body, and a wide open rim. Named for its use in carrying melon-sized produce. A good follow-up project after a round base: introduces straight-sided randing and a paired-rod border.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for round-basket construction. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the melon-basket form and garden use.

glossaryTerms.definition: Willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes.
A tight basketry weave that strengthens transition points.
Basketry weave in which two or more rods are woven together as one, for speed and weight on large areas.

content.text: A melon basket is a wide, straight-sided round basket about 30 cm in diameter and 20 cm deep. The straight sides make it easier than a tapering shape for a second willow project: there is no angle to manage, only consistent
randing
to the required height.
Slewing
(weaving two rods as one) speeds up the body of the basket.
Soaking

Soak all rods for twelve to twenty-four hours before working. Brown willow (dried-in-bark willow) needs longer than buff willow (peeled and dried). Test by attempting to bend a rod through a tight arc: if it bends without cracking, it is ready; if it snaps, soak for another four hours.
The round base

Lay six thick base sticks horizontally. Pierce the centre of three of them with a bodkin and thread the other three through the holes to form a cross. Pair-weave two thin rods around the cross for two circuits, separating the sticks into individual spokes as you go. Then rand with single rods until the base reaches 30 cm.
Finish with a
waling
border row around the base perimeter.
Staking up
Insert side stakes beside each base spoke. Use a thick rod (6 mm) for each stake. Bend each stake upright at the waling border. Tie the tops temporarily and begin side weaving.
The sides

Work three rows of waling first to lock the stakes upright. Then slew: take two rods together and rand them as one over-and-under. Two rods together fill the row faster and produce a dense, strong sidewall. Continue slewing to 18 cm height.
Finish with three more rows of waling to stiffen the rim area before the border.
The border

Work a four-rod border for a stronger, wider finish on a large basket. Take four consecutive stakes, bend each behind the next, and weave a four-step sequence before trimming. The four-rod border produces a rope-like finish.
Variations
Side handles: fit two loop handles at opposite points of the rim using wrapped rod pairs.
Smaller display basket: reduce to 20 cm diameter. Reduce the number of base sticks by two.

rows.symptom: Sides are not straight, they taper inward toward the top
rows.cause: Stake tops were pulled in when tied during staking-up
rows.fix: Untie the top. Use a stick held across the stakes at mid-height to hold the intended diameter while establishing the first three rows of side weaving.

rows.symptom: Slewing rows look loose compared to randing rows
rows.cause: Two rods not kept side by side during weaving
rows.fix: Keep the two rods lying flat next to each other, not crossing. Press each row down firmly with the rapping iron before adding the next.
